summary:
The Nigerian Civil War, also known as the Biafran War, was fought from 1967 to 1970 after the predominantly Igbo southeastern region of Nigeria declared independence as the Republic of Biafra on May 30, 1967. The conflict arose from deep ethnic, religious, and political tensions in Nigeria following independence in 1960 — particularly after coups in 1966, massacres of Igbo people in northern Nigeria, and the failure of political negotiations. Colonel Odumegwu Ojukwu led the Biafran declaration while the Nigerian federal government under General Yakubu Gowon moved to suppress the secession.

The war was marked by a comprehensive blockade of Biafra that caused severe famine. Images of starving Biafran children reached international audiences through unprecedented television coverage, galvanizing public opinion across the world and triggering one of the first large-scale international humanitarian responses. France, Portugal, and some African states supported Biafra, while Britain, the Soviet Union, and most other powers backed the federal government.

Biafra surrendered in January 1970 after catastrophic losses. Estimates of deaths from combat and famine range from one to three million. Gowon pursued a policy of reconciliation rather than retribution, and Nigeria reintegrated the Igbo population with notable success. The war remains a formative event in Nigerian national consciousness and significantly shaped international humanitarian norms around access to civilian populations in conflict zones.
